evidence: Chowla's cosine problem asks whether some theta makes the cosine sum over A, sum of cos(n*theta), drop below -c*N^(1/2).
verify_note: Decl exists in 510.lean; the statement is literally the cosine sum ∑ cos(nθ) (Chowla's cosine problem); label matches Q1256164.

evidence: The conjectured bound -c*N^(1/2) is stated via sqrt N in the Lean statement.
verify_note: sqrt N appears in the statement as the conjectured bound -c*√N, the core content of the conjecture.

evidence: The statement quantifies over finite sets A of positive integers of size N (Finset in the Lean statement).
verify_note: Statement quantifies over A : Finset ℕ with #A = N, so finite sets are load-bearing.

evidence: Ruzsa's solved variant bounds the negative values of the cosine sum sum of cos(n*theta) over A.
verify_note: Decl erdos_510.variants.ruzsa exists; its statement is again the cosine sum ∑ cos(nθ) over A.

evidence: Ruzsa's upper bound has the form -exp(c*sqrt(log N)) in the statement.
verify_note: Ruzsa bound in the statement is -exp(c * sqrt (log N)), so the exponential function is literal and load-bearing.

evidence: The bound -exp(c*sqrt(log N)) involves the logarithm of the set size N.
verify_note: log N appears inside Ruzsa's bound -exp(c*√(log N)) in the Lean statement.

evidence: Bedert's solved variant proves the cosine sum over A is below -c*N^(1/7) for some theta.
verify_note: Decl erdos_510.variants.bedert exists; statement bounds the cosine sum by -c*N^(1/7) as claimed.

evidence: The statement quantifies over finite sets A of positive integers of size N.
verify_note: Bedert variant quantifies over A : Finset ℕ of size N; finite sets load-bearing.

evidence: The problem concerns random polynomials f(z) with coefficients drawn uniformly from {-1,1} (Kac polynomials).
verify_note: Decl exists in 522.lean; KacCoefficients.polynomial random polynomials with {-1,1} coefficients are the object of the statement.

evidence: R_n counts the roots of f(z) in the closed unit disk {z in C : |z| <= 1} (Metric.closedBall 0 1).
verify_note: numRootsInUnitDisk counts roots in Metric.closedBall 0 1, i.e. the closed unit disk; literal in the statement.

evidence: The question is whether R_n/(n/2) tends to 1 almost surely, formalized as a probability-one event.
verify_note: Statement asserts ℙ{ω | ...Tendsto...} = 1, the formalization of almost-sure convergence; docstring says 'almost surely'.

evidence: The roots are counted in the complex unit disk, with coefficients in Set C.
verify_note: Coefficient set is {-1,1} : Set ℂ and roots are counted in ℂ; complex numbers are the ambient domain.

evidence: The KacCoefficients hypothesis requires the coefficients to be independent uniformly distributed random variables (iIndepFun).
verify_note: KacCoefficients bundles iIndepFun (independence) as hypothesis h_indep; Q625303 is the canonical Wikidata item for probabilistic independence (label verified).

evidence: The variant concerns random polynomials with coefficients drawn uniformly from {0,1}.
verify_note: Decl erdos_522.variants.zero_one exists; random polynomial with {0,1} coefficients is the statement's object.

evidence: R_n counts roots of the random polynomial in {z in C : |z| <= 1}.
verify_note: Variant counts roots via numRootsInUnitDisk (Metric.closedBall 0 1).

evidence: The variant asks whether R_n/(n/2) converges to 1 almost surely.
verify_note: Statement is ℙ{convergence of 2R_n/n to 1} = 1, i.e. almost-sure convergence.

evidence: Erdos and Offord counted real roots of a random degree-n polynomial with plus-minus-1 coefficients.
verify_note: Decl erdos_522.variants.number_real_roots exists; real-rooted random ±1 polynomial is the object (KacCoefficients over ℝ).

evidence: The statement counts the number of real roots (f.roots n) of the random polynomial.
verify_note: Statement counts (f.roots n ω).card, the real roots/zeros of the random polynomial; label verified.

evidence: The root count is (2/pi + o(1)) log n, with (n : R).log appearing in the Lean statement.
verify_note: (n : ℝ).log appears literally in the root-count normalization of the statement.

evidence: The asymptotic constant is 2/pi in the Erdos-Offord count of real roots.
verify_note: The constant 2/π appears literally in the statement as the Erdős–Offord asymptotic constant.

evidence: Yakir proved almost all Kac polynomials have n/2 + O(n^(9/10)) roots in the unit disk.
verify_note: Decl erdos_522.variants.yakir_solution exists; Kac polynomials are the statement's object.

evidence: The root count is over {z in C : |z| <= 1}, i.e. membership in Metric.closedBall 0 1.
verify_note: Root count is countP (· ∈ Metric.closedBall 0 1), the closed unit disk in ℂ.

evidence: The statement counts roots of the random polynomial with multiplicity (f.roots n).
verify_note: Statement counts (f.roots n ω) with multiplicity inside the disk; zeros of the polynomial are load-bearing.

evidence: The problem is about a family F of sets closed under taking subsets and its intersecting subfamilies F'.
verify_note: Decl exists in 701.lean; F : Set (Set X) closed under subsets with intersecting subfamilies — family of sets is the central object.

evidence: Closure under taking subsets is formalized as IsLowerSet F, i.e. F is a lower set in the subset order.
verify_note: Statement hypothesis is IsLowerSet F; Q2528262 (label 'upper set', verified) is the Wikidata item for the Upper-and-lower-sets article covering the dual lower-set notion used here.

evidence: The problem asks whether every infinite proportionately dissociated set A (A.Infinite) is a finite union of dissociated sets.
verify_note: Decl Erdos774.erdos_774 exists; hypothesis A.Infinite makes infinite sets load-bearing.

evidence: Dissociated means distinct finite subsets X, Y of A always have distinct sums, and every finite B in A must contain a large dissociated subset.
verify_note: IsDissociated is defined via finite subsets (Finset ℕ) with distinct sums, and proportionate dissociation quantifies over finite B ⊆ A.

evidence: The conclusion asserts A equals the union of a finite collection T of dissociated sets (A = sUnion T).
verify_note: Conclusion is A = ⋃₀ T with T finite — a finite union of dissociated sets, literal in the statement.

evidence: Dissociated sets, defined by pairwise-distinct finite subset sums, are a standard notion of additive combinatorics.
verify_note: Merely thematic field tag: 'additive combinatorics' appears nowhere in the statement or docstring; the AMS class is 5 (combinatorics) and the concrete notions are already tagged separately.

evidence: h(n) is defined over sets A of n integers (Finset Z) whose subset sums coincide only for equal cardinalities.
verify_note: Decl exists in 789.lean; IsSubsetSumSeparatingCard quantifies over A : Finset ℤ, sets of n integers.

evidence: The problem asks to estimate h(n), interpreted as determining Theta(h(n)).
verify_note: The statement is the Θ-estimate (subsetSumThreshold) =Θ[atTop] answer(sorry); 'Estimate h(n)' is itself an asymptotic-estimation problem.

evidence: The estimate is stated with the Theta asymptotic notation =Theta[atTop].
verify_note: The statement uses the Bachmann–Landau notation =Θ[atTop], covered by the big O notation item.

evidence: The variant asks whether h(n) = Theta(sqrt n).
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.sq exists; √n is literally the conjectured order in the statement.

evidence: The growth claim is stated via the Theta notation =Theta[atTop].
verify_note: Statement uses =Θ[atTop] notation.

evidence: Straus proved the upper bound h(n) << sqrt n.
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.isBigO_sq exists; √n is the Straus upper bound in the statement, matching docstring [Str66].

evidence: The bound is stated as =O[atTop], i.e. h(n) = O(sqrt n).
verify_note: Statement uses =O[atTop] notation, i.e. h(n) = O(√n).

evidence: The open variant asks for the complementary bound sqrt n = O(h(n)).
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.sq_isBigO exists; statement is √n =O[atTop] h(n), the open complementary bound.

evidence: The bound is stated as =O[atTop] between sqrt n and h(n).
verify_note: Statement uses =O[atTop] between √n and subsetSumThreshold.

evidence: The variant asks whether h(n) = Theta((n log n)^(1/3)), with Real.log n in the statement.
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.cube_root_linearithmic exists; Real.log n appears literally in (n * Real.log n)^(1/3).

evidence: The growth claim is stated via the Theta notation =Theta[atTop].
verify_note: Statement uses =Θ[atTop] notation.

evidence: Erdos and Choi proved the lower bound (n log n)^(1/3) << h(n).
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.cube_root_linearithmic_isBigO exists; Real.log in the Erdős–Choi lower bound, matching docstring [Er62c][Ch74b].

evidence: The bound is stated as (n log n)^(1/3) = O(h(n)) via =O[atTop].
verify_note: Statement is (n log n)^(1/3) =O[atTop] h(n).

evidence: The open variant asks whether h(n) = O((n log n)^(1/3)).
verify_note: Decl erdos_789.variants.isBigO_cube_root_linearithmic exists; Real.log appears in the open bound h(n) = O((n log n)^(1/3)).

evidence: The bound is stated via the big-O notation =O[atTop].
verify_note: Statement uses =O[atTop] notation.

evidence: The problem asks for distinct a, b, c in A whose pairwise sums a+b, a+c, b+c all land back in A.
verify_note: Decl exists in 865.lean; the statement's core condition is a+b, a+c, b+c ∈ A — pairwise (restricted-sumset) sums landing in the set.

evidence: The density threshold 5N/8 + C forces A in {1,...,N} to contain a triple with all pairwise sums in A, the opposite of being sum-free.
verify_note: Tangential: the statement concerns a triple with all pairwise sums in A (k=3 configuration), not sum-freeness; 'opposite of being sum-free' overstates — the concept appears nowhere in statement or docstring.

evidence: This Erdos-Sos problem on pairwise sums inside dense subsets of {1,...,N} is an additive-combinatorics question (AMS 5 11).
verify_note: Merely thematic field tag: evidence cites only the AMS class; 'additive combinatorics' appears nowhere in the statement or docstring.

evidence: The k=2 case finds distinct a, b in A with a+b in A whenever |A| >= N+2 in {1,...,2N}.
verify_note: Decl erdos_865.variants.k2 exists; statement produces distinct a, b with a+b ∈ A — pairwise-sum-in-set condition as claimed.

evidence: The folklore fact says a subset of {1,...,2N} of size N+2 cannot avoid containing a pair with its sum, i.e. cannot be sum-free with distinct summands.
verify_note: The k2 statement is exactly the contrapositive of the extremal bound for sum-free-with-distinct-summands subsets of {1,...,2N}, so the concept is load-bearing.

evidence: Erdos and Sos conjectured the asymptotic f_k(N) ~ (1/2)(1 + sum of 4^(-r)) N, stated with the ~[atTop] equivalence.
verify_note: Decl erdos_865.variants.sos exists; the statement is the asymptotic equivalence f_k(N) ~[atTop] (1/2)(1+∑ 4^-r)N — asymptotics are the content.

evidence: f_k(N) is the minimal size guaranteeing k elements whose pairwise sums all lie in the set.
verify_note: f N k is defined by ∃ S of size k with all pairwise sums x+y ∈ A; the sumset condition is the definition's core.

evidence: Choi, Erdos, and Szemeredi proved f_k(N) <= (2/3 - epsilon_k) N for large N, an eventual asymptotic bound.
verify_note: Tangential: the statement has only an eventual bound (∀ᶠ N in atTop, f ≤ (2/3-ε)N) with no asymptotic notation; a bare eventually-quantifier does not make 'asymptotic analysis' load-bearing.

evidence: The quantity f_k(N) bounded here counts thresholds for k elements with all pairwise sums in the set.
verify_note: Decl erdos_865.variants.upper_bound exists; the bounded quantity f N k is defined via pairwise sums lying in the set.

evidence: The problem asks whether 2^x and 3^x both being integers forces the real exponent x to be an integer.
verify_note: Decl exists in 17560.lean; the hypotheses are 2^x and 3^x with real exponent x — exponentiation is the problem's substance.

evidence: Both the hypotheses (2^x and 3^x are integers) and the conclusion (x is an integer) are integrality statements.
verify_note: Hypotheses (∃ m : ℕ, 2^x = m, 3^x = m) and conclusion (∃ m : ℕ, x = m) are integrality statements as the docstring says.

evidence: The exponent x ranges over the real numbers ({x : R} in the statement).
verify_note: The exponent is {x : ℝ}; real numbers are the ambient domain of the problem.

evidence: The variant assumes n^x is an integer for every natural number n and concludes x is an integer.
verify_note: Decl mathoverflow_17560.variants.all_nats exists; hypothesis is n^x integral for every n.

evidence: The hypothesis and conclusion are integrality conditions on the powers n^x and on x.
verify_note: Hypothesis and conclusion are integrality of n^x and of x.

evidence: The variant assumes 2^x, 3^x and 5^x are all integers and concludes x is an integer.
verify_note: Decl mathoverflow_17560.variants.with_5 exists; hypotheses are 2^x, 3^x, 5^x all integral.

evidence: The hypotheses and conclusion are integrality statements about 2^x, 3^x, 5^x and x.
verify_note: Integrality of 2^x, 3^x, 5^x and of x is the hypothesis/conclusion structure.

evidence: The conjecture is about fusible numbers, defined as the smallest set of rationals (Q) containing 0 and closed under the fuse operation (a+b+1)/2.
verify_note: Decl FusibleNumber.conj_7_1 exists; IsFusible : ℚ → Prop defines fusible numbers as rationals closed under (a+b+1)/2 from 0.

evidence: The statement divides [x+1, y+1) into half-open intervals [l_n, l_(n+1)) (Set.Ico) classifying the fusible numbers there.
verify_note: Statement classifies fusible q ∈ Set.Ico (ℓ n) (ℓ (n+1)), the half-open intervals tiling [x+1, y+1) per the docstring.

evidence: The declaration formalizes Conjecture 7.1 of Erickson, Nivasch, and Xu's paper 'Fusible numbers and Peano Arithmetic'.
verify_note: Tangential: Peano Arithmetic appears only in the cited paper's title; the conj_7_1 statement and docstring are purely about fusible-number structure with no PA content.
